Embark on a virtual journey to the vibrant city of Limassol, Cyprus, as we unveil the top things to do and see in this coastal gem. This immersive video will take you on a tour of Limassol's rich history, stunning beaches, and exciting attractions.

Explore Limassol's captivating historical sites that showcase the city's fascinating past. Visit the Limassol Castle, a medieval fortress where Richard the Lionheart is said to have married Queen Berengaria. Immerse yourself in the ancient ruins of Kourion, an archaeological site that features well-preserved Greco-Roman theaters and mosaics.

Indulge in the sun-soaked beaches of Limassol and enjoy the crystal-clear waters of the Mediterranean Sea. Relax on the golden sands of Ladies' Mile Beach or take part in thrilling water sports activities such as jet skiing, paddleboarding, or parasailing.

Discover the vibrant culture and lively atmosphere of Limassol's Old Town. Wander through its narrow streets lined with traditional houses and colorful shops. Visit the vibrant Limassol Marina, where you can admire luxurious yachts, dine in waterfront restaurants, and enjoy a lively nightlife scene.

Immerse yourself in the local cuisine and taste the flavors of Cyprus. Sample traditional dishes like souvlaki, halloumi cheese, and moussaka at local taverns and restaurants. Don't forget to indulge in the famous Cypriot wine, produced in the nearby vineyards.

Experience the excitement of Limassol's festivals and events. If you're visiting during the Carnival season, be prepared for colorful parades and festivities. Explore the Limassol Wine Festival, where you can taste a variety of local wines and enjoy live music and performances.

Join us as we uncover the hidden gems and must-visit attractions of Limassol, the cosmopolitan heart of Cyprus. Let us transport you to a world of ancient wonders, sun-kissed beaches, and warm Cypriot hospitality.

Get ready to be captivated by the allure of Limassol in this immersive video experience. So, pack your bags, and let the adventure begin in this remarkable coastal city where history, culture, and relaxation blend seamlessly!

videos.description:
Welcome to another video!

In this episode we are going to Limassol in Cyprus and I will show you the Top 7 Things to See and Do in Limassol. 🌴🏰😎☀️

Limassol is a vibrant coastal city on the southern coast of Cyprus, located in the south-eastern corner of Europe. There are international airports in Paphos and Larnaca. They are both roughly a 45 minutes drive from Limassol. 

Limassol offers a sunny Mediterranean climate with hot summers and mild winters. A bustling seafront promenade, lined with cafes and restaurants dominates the city. It also has a beautiful old town and a modern Marina. The city's rich heritage is showcased through landmarks like Limassol Castle, dating back to the medieval period. Limassol also hosts an array of festivals and events, including the colorful Limassol Carnival during spring-time.

Come along on this tour of the city and I will show you all of my favorite things in Limassol. 

Please like and subscribe to the channel to see more content like this.

MY TOP 7 - THINGS TO DO AND SEE IN LIMASSOL - CYPRUS

1. Limassol Marina
2. Agia Napa Church
3. Castle of Limassol
4. Limassol Old Town
5. Omodos Village
6. Molos Sea Promenade
7. Limassol Carnival

videos.title: 16 Best Things To Do in Limassol | Cyprus Travel Guide
videos.description:
If you're looking for a destination that combines history, culture, nature and adventure, look no further than Limassol, Cyprus. 

Limassol is the second-largest city in Cyprus and a major tourist hub, offering something for everyone. In this video, we'll show you 16 of the best things to do in Limassol and its surroundings, from exploring the city's landmarks and attractions to discovering the stunning coast and mountains.

Timestamps:
- Intro - 0:00
THE CITY
- Limassol Promenade - 0:30
- Limassol Castle - 0:50
- Limassol Marina - 1:19
- Ayia Napa Cathedral - 1:38
THE COAST
- Kourion Archaeological Site - 1:58
- Sanctuary of Apollo Hylates - 2:47
 - Akrotiri Shipwreck - 3:07
- Cape Aspro - 4:05
- Old Church of Saint Nicholaos (Alassa) - 5:08
- Omodos - 5:45
THE MOUNTAINS
- Troodos Mountains - 6:21
- Pouziaris Nature Trail - 6:56
- Milia Bridge - 7:26
- Caledonia Waterfalls - 7:49
- Millomeris Waterfall - 8:19
- Artemis Trail - 8:31
- Outro - 9:25

Link to full Cyprus Playlist:
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=YjgRmMT71gI&list=PLoliWvnVweHue4Lz2tPUUdQOvvRJdL4Yi

THE CITY
- Limassol Promenade 
 Enjoy a relaxing stroll along the Limassol Promenade, a scenic waterfront walkway that stretches for several kilometers along the Mediterranean Sea. You'll find plenty of cafes, restaurants, bars and shops along the way, as well as parks, playgrounds and sculptures. 
- Limassol Castle
 Visit the Limassol Castle, a medieval fortress that dates back to the 13th century and was once the residence of the Lusignan kings of Cyprus. You can also climb to the top of the castle for a panoramic view of the city and the harbor.
- Limassol Marina
 Experience the luxury and glamour of the Limassol Marina, a modern development that features a marina with yachts and boats, a shopping and dining complex, and residential villas and apartments. 
- Ayia Napa Cathedral
 Admire the architecture and art of the Ayia Napa Cathedral, a Greek Orthodox church that was built in the 19th century on the site of an older chapel. 

THE COAST
- Kourion Archaeological Site
 Step back in time at the Kourion Archaeological Site, one of the most impressive ancient sites in Cyprus. Kourion was an ancient city-state that flourished from the 8th century BC to the 4th century AD, and was destroyed by an earthquake in 365 AD.
- Sanctuary of Apollo Hylates
 Discover the Sanctuary of Apollo Hylates, a sacred site dedicated to Apollo, the god of light, music and prophecy.
- Akrotiri Shipwreck
 Witness the Akrotiri Shipwreck, a cargo ship that ran aground on the Akrotiri Peninsula in 1974 due to bad weather. 
- Cape Aspro
 Marvel at Cape Aspro, a spectacular cliff formation that juts out into the sea on the south coast of Cyprus. Cape Aspro means "white cape" in Greek.
- Old Church of Saint Nicholaos (Alassa)
 Visit the Old Church of Saint Nicholaos (Alassa), a Byzantine church that was built in the 12th century and is now submerged under water due to the construction of a dam in 1988. 
- Omodos
 Explore Omodos, a picturesque village that is located in the foothills of Troodos Mountains. Omodos is known for its wine production. You can wander the cobblestone streets, visit the Monastery of the Holy Cross, and shop for handicrafts and souvenirs.

THE MOUNTAINS
- Troodos Mountains
 Experience the beauty and diversity of the Troodos Mountains, the largest mountain range in Cyprus that covers most of the island's interior. The Troodos Mountains offer a variety of activities and attractions for all seasons, such as hiking, biking, skiing, snowshoeing, camping, and birdwatching. 
- Pouziaris Nature Trail
 Hike the Pouziaris Nature Trail, a circular trail that starts and ends at the Troodos Square. The trail is about 8 km long
- Caledonia Waterfalls
 See the Caledonia Waterfalls, one of the highest and most beautiful waterfalls in Cyprus. The waterfalls are located near Platres and are accessible by a hiking trail that follows the river. 
- Artemis Trail
 Challenge yourself on the Artemis Trail, a circular trail that starts and ends at the Olympus peak, the highest point in Cyprus at 1952 meters above sea level. It offers breathtaking views of the island.

We hope you enjoyed this video and found some inspiration for your next trip to Limassol, Cyprus. Limassol is a city that has it all: history, culture, nature and adventure. Don't forget to like, comment and subscribe for more travel videos from around the world. Thanks for watching!

videos.transcript: Welcome to Limassol, Cyprus’ largest seaport and the country’s second-largest city, located on the southern coast along Akrotiri Bay. It serves as a major commercial hub but is also a key stop for cruise ships visiting the island. The city is known for its modern marina, historic old town, and easy access to some of Cyprus’ most important archaeological sites and beautiful beaches. In this video, I’ll take you through the top things to do in Limassol and the best places to explore in Cyprus during your time in port. Cruise ships dock at Limassol's New Port, located about 4 km (2.5 miles) from the city center, a 10-minute drive away. The Limassol Cruise Terminal within the port is modern and well-equipped, offering passenger services, duty-free shopping, and transportation options. Taxis, shuttles, and local bus line #30 are available to take visitors into Limassol’s old town and marina. Start your exploration with Limassol’s Old Port, now part of the Limassol Marina, a vibrant waterfront area filled with restaurants, cafés, and shops. Once a traditional fishing harbor, it has been transformed into a modern marina where you can walk along the waterfront, admire luxury yachts, and take in views of historic landmarks while enjoying the relaxed seaside atmosphere. If you’re visiting Cyprus in July or August like I did, don’t be surprised to find the city quieter during the day, as temperatures can reach up to 40°C (104°F), with many locals escaping to the mountains or the beach. From the marina, continue to Molos Promenade, a scenic waterfront walkway stretching along the coast. This beautifully landscaped area features palm-lined paths, parks, fountains and art installations, making it a perfect spot for a leisurely stroll or a bike ride. With stunning sea views and plenty of benches to relax, it’s a favorite place for both locals and visitors to enjoy the Mediterranean breeze. From there, right across the street, you can access Limassol’s Old Town, the historic heart of the city. This lively district is home to narrow streets lined with colorful buildings, raditional tavernas, and boutique shops, offering a mix of old and new. Nearby, you’ll find Anexartisias Street, the city’s main shopping area, along with the medieval Limassol Castle, a historic landmark in the heart of the Old Town. The castle dates back to 1590, though the original 12th-century structure was later rebuilt during Ottoman rule. According to tradition, this is where Richard the Lionheart married Berengaria of Navarre, marking the first royal marriage of an English king outside England. Today, the castle houses the Medieval Collection of the Cyprus Museum, showcasing artifacts from Cyprus’ rich history. The Agia Napa Cathedral is another Old Town landmark, and one of the city's most important Orthodox churches. Built in the early 20th century on the site of an older chapel, it features impressive Byzantine-style architecture, intricate frescoes, and a beautifully decorated interior. Akti Olympion Beach is Limassol’s main city beach, stretching along the coastline around 2 km from Limassol Marina. You can find more details about beaches near Limassol on my blog Adventourbegins.com, but later in the video, I’ll take you to some of Cyprus’ best beaches in Ayia Napa, where I stayed during my visit. If you plan to venture beyond Limassol and explore Cyprus’ top attractions, which I highly recommend, consider booking a guided tour or renting a car, just keep in mind that driving is on the left side of the road. These are my top recommendations for places to visit in Cyprus, to make the most of your trip. Cyprus has a rich history dating back over 10,000 years, influenced by civilizations including the Greeks, Romans, Byzantines, and Ottomans. The island is dotted with remarkable archaeological sites, including the ancient cities of Kourion and Paphos, which are well worth a visit during your stay. Located about an hour’s drive from Limassol, the Archaeological Site of Nea Paphos, is one of Cyprus’ most significant historical landmarks and a UNESCO World Heritage Site. Paphos has been inhabited since the Neolithic period and was a major center for the cult of Aphrodite, the Greek goddess of love, who, according to legend, was born along its shores. The site is home to well-preserved Roman villas, intricate mosaics, theaters and tombs, offering a glimpse into the island’s ancient past. Among its highlights are the Tombs of the Kings, an underground necropolis carved into rock, and the stunning mosaics of the House of Dionysos, depicting mythological scenes. While in Paphos, be sure to visit the medieval Paphos Castle and explore the vibrant harbor area, or, if you’re a beach lover, relax on one of the area’s stunning beaches. Located just a 25-minute drive from Limassol’s port, Kourion is another impressive archaeological site, offering stunning views from its hilltop location. Once a powerful city-kingdom, Kourion thrived until a devastating earthquake in 365 AD. The site’s highlight is the Kourion Ancient Amphitheater, originally built in the 2nd century BC, which still hosts open-air performances today. Visitors can also explore the House of Eustolios, with its well-preserved mosaics, the House of the Gladiators, the Early Christian Basilica, and other fascinating ruins from the Roman and Byzantine periods. Finally, if you're interested in medieval history, Kolossi Castle, located just outside Limassol, is a well-preserved 13th-century fortress, that once served as a base for the Knights of St. John. Between Kourion and Paphos, some tours make a stop at Aphrodite’s Rock (Petra tou Romiou), a famous sea stack along the coast. According to legend, this is the mythical birthplace of Aphrodite, the Greek goddess of love and beauty. The area offers a stunning seaside view, and many visitors stop to take photos or even swim near the rocks, as local myths say it brings good luck and eternal youth. Beach lovers will find plenty to enjoy in Cyprus, known for its crystal-clear waters and stunning coastline. While Akti Olympion Beach and Governor’s Beach are the closest options to Limassol, the island’s most beautiful beaches are on the east coast in Ayia Napa and Protaras. Here, you’ll find breathtaking spots like Nissi Beach, Landa Beach and Makronissos Beach, some of my personal favorites. Ayia Napa is about a 1.5-hour drive from Limassol Port, with no direct bus connection. The best way to get there is by taxi, private transfer, or an organized excursion. Located on Cyprus’ east coast, Ayia Napa is renowned for its white sandy beaches, crystal-clear waters, and vibrant atmosphere. It boasts some of the island’s most stunning beaches, including Nissi Beach, Makronissos Beach, and Landa Beach, all known for their soft sands and turquoise waters. Nissi Beach, the most popular beach in Cyprus, gets extremely crowded, so arriving early is recommended. All beaches are well-equipped with facilities, sunbeds and beachfront restaurants, where you can enjoy both local and international cuisine. Beyond the beaches, Ayia Napa offers plenty to explore, from the historic Agia Napa Monastery to the scenic trails of Cape Greco National Park. You can also go shopping, dining, or take a boat cruise from Ayia Napa Marina, where tours explore sea caves, hidden coves, and offer snorkeling, swimming, and even sunset sailing experiences along the coast. For those interested in history and Cyprus’ Turkish heritage, a visit to Nicosia, the island’s divided capital, is a must. The city is split between the Republic of Cyprus and the self-declared Turkish Republic of Northern Cyprus, with the Green Line running through its center. You can explore Ledra Street, the Venetian Walls, Buyuk Han and Selimiye Mosque, offering a fascinating mix of cultures. If you plan to cross into Northern Cyprus, make sure to carry your passport or visa if required. Finally, if you want to escape the coast, head to the Troodos Mountains, home to scenic villages, nature trails, and UNESCO-listed Byzantine churches and monasteries. One of the most remarkable is the 12th-century Holy Church of Panagia of Asinou, famous for its well-preserved Byzantine frescoes. You can also visit the Kykkos Monastery, the most famous in Cyprus, known for its rich history and impressive gold-adorned icons. And while in Cyprus, do not miss tasting fresh seafood by the marina, traditional meze at a local taverna, or loukoumades honey-soaked doughnuts for a sweet treat. Cypriot cuisine is all about fresh seafood and grilled meats like the souvlaki, gyros and halloumi, along with fresh salads and classic dips like tzatziki and hummus. For more places to visit and things to do in Cyprus from Limassol Port, check out my blog Adventourbegins.com. You can also find links in the description to book tours and services for your trip. Thank you for watching, and don’t forget to like, subscribe, and drop a comment if you have any questions. Safe travels!

videos.transcript: [Music] Limol blends coastal charm with rich history, vibrant night life and Mediterranean elegance. From ancient ruins to luxury marinas, this criate city offers a perfect mix of culture and relaxation. Stroll its palmline prominade or explore its lively old town vibes. [Music] In this video, you'll see the top 10 things to see and do in Limol. [Music] The suggestions are based on our several exciting trips to the city. Here are our top 10 picks. [Music] Starting with number one, Limos Marina. Limosal Marina is a dazzling fusion of luxury living and Mediterranean charm, making it the city's most iconic destination. This upscale waterfront combines modern apartments, elegant yachts. Visitors can enjoy fine dining, stroll along the prominade, or book a relaxing boat tour. Whether you're sipping coffee with a view or indulging in retail therapy, the atmosphere remains vibrant and elegant. Its seamless blend of lifestyle, leisure, and design makes it a magnet for tourists, locals, and influencers alike, offering a taste of modern Cyprus in one perfect setting. [Music] Moving on to number two, Limos Castle and Medieval Museum. Limos Castle sits in the heart of the old town and holds centuries of rich history. Originally built in the Middle Ages and later modified by the Ottomans, this fortress is said to be the site of Richard the Lionheart's wedding. Today, it houses the medieval Museum of Cypress, showcasing armor, pottery, coins, and relics that transport visitors through time. The fixed stone walls and narrow corridors offer an authentic medieval atmosphere. Climb to the roof for panoramic city views. With both historical significance and architectural charm, it's a must- vvisit site for history lovers and cultural explorers alike. [Music] Number three, Molo's Prominade. Molo's Prominade is a scenic waterfront pathway beloved by locals and tourists alike. This urban park features palm trees and biking paths, all set against a beautiful coastal view. It's perfect for walks, jogging, or sunset photos. Street vendors and events often energize the area, especially on weekends. The mix of natural beauty and public art makes it visually appealing. Whether enjoying quiet moments or soaking in the city's vibe, Molo offers a refreshing escape in Limosol's buzzing seaside heart. Number four, Fazoui Water Mania Water Park. Fazoui Water Mania is Cypress's largest water park and one of Limosol's top family attractions. Spanning an impressive area, it features a thrilling variety of water slides, wave pools, a lazy river, and splash zones designed for all age groups. The tropical style setting with palm trees and thatched huts adds a holiday vibe. There are plenty of shaded areas and even massage zones for those seeking relaxation. Lifeguards ensure a safe environment making it ideal for families. Whether you're looking for adrenaline pumping slides or tranquil floating, Fazoi offers hours of aquatic fun under the Mediterranean sun. [Music] Number five in our list, Dudi Beach. Dudi Beach is Limol's favorite urban beach, combining nature and convenience. Located in the city, it features soft sand, clean waters, and shade from towering eucalyptus trees nearby. The calm leaves make it perfect for swimmers of all ages. Fully equipped with sunbeds, showers, a jogging and cycling path runs along the shore. Whether sunbathing, swimming, or enjoying seaside snacks, Dudi offers a true Mediterranean beach experience that's easy to reach and loved by both locals and tourists. [Music] Number six, Limol Zoo. [Music] Limosal Zoo is a compact and well-kept spot near the city's prominade. Renovated with care, it houses birds, monkeys, and mammals and shaded enclosures. Informative signs and interactive zones make it great for families. A mini natural history museum and small petting area are included. Its peaceful setting by the sea makes it ideal for a short visit. It's an easygoing destination that blends nature and learning. Whether you're traveling with children or just seeking a relaxing hour outdoors, the zoo adds charm to any limosol day. [Music] Number seven, Myal Limol. My Limol is the city's biggest shopping and entertainment center located near Fazoui. The mall offers international fashion, electronics, home stores, and a spacious food court. Beyond shopping, it features an ice rink, arcade games. Its large air conditioned layout provides a great break from the Mediterranean heat. Whether looking for trendy styles, indoor fun, or a casual bite, My Mall gives visitors a convenient blend of retail and leisure in a clean, modern venue that keeps everyone happily occupied. [Music] Number eight, Archaeological Museum of Limol District. The archaeological museum of Limol offers a journey through Cypress's ancient history. Near the city center, it displays artifacts from the Neolithic to Roman eras. Visitors see pottery, tools, statues, and jewelry from local digs. Roman mosaics and terracotta figures highlight Cypress's legacy. The museum's quiet setting appeals to history lovers. Its collection is compact but rich, offering cultural depth without being overwhelming. This often overlooked gem adds valuable context to Limol's past and gives curious travelers a deeper look at the island's historical treasures. [Music] Number nine, Cypress Motor Museum. The Cypress Motor Museum is a unique destination for auto lovers and casual visitors alike. Home to over 120 vintage and rare cars, it spans from 1912 models to modern racers. Founded by rally champion Dei Maropolis, it includes themed exhibitions and even movie famous cars like Mr. Bean's Mini. Every vehicle is displayed with detailed history. It's not just about engines, it's about design, culture, and nostalgia. Located in absonas, it adds a mechanical twist to Limosol's culture scene and offers one of the city's most engaging and unusual indoor attractions. [Music] Finally, number 10, Calonia Waterfall. Calonia Waterfall, located in the Trudeau's foothills within Limal District, is a peaceful natural getaway. A short, shady hiking trail winds through pine forests and wooden bridges to reach the cascading falls, dropping from a height of 12 m. The cool air and sound of water create a calm atmosphere. It's perfect for a half-day nature trip away from city life. Though just outside Limol's core, it's still in the district and easily accessible. Nature lovers, photographers, and couples will all enjoy this scenic forest walk and tranquil reward at the end. Now it's time to hear from you. What's your favorite thing to do in Limasol? Is there something we missed? Let us know in the comments section below. And don't forget to give this video a like and subscribe to Greenable if you haven't already. Click the bell icon to stay updated on all our latest content. [Music]
